turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument now in number oh oh six five six seven, Larry Dean Dusenbery versus the United States. Mr. Zieve Ms. Zieve. Pardon. [Inaudible]

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. The issue in this case is whether the procedures used to serve notice of forfeiture of petitioner's property satisfied due process. The Federal Government forfeited Mr. Dusenbery's twenty-two thousand dollars in nineteen eighty-eight after mailing a notice to him at the Federal prison where he was incarcerated. It's not disputed that he did not get the notice. The governor the Government argues that its nineteen eighty-eight procedures satisfied due process whether or not the notice was received. However, in light of the circumstances presented here, including the Government's control over the prisoner's location and knowledge of the prisoner's location at all times and its control over prison procedures, due process requires the Government to use procedures that offer assurances of delivery to the inmate addressee and not just to prison personnel. Such procedures...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well, you take the position that it's not sufficient that there be staff at the prison who deliver mail to the inmates?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: That, in and of itself, is not sufficient. There the the procedures need...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: A procedure... I ne-

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: whereby a prison employee delivers certified mail to prisoners is inadequate in your...

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: That that, in and of itself, is inadequate.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: although...

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Is inadequate, although...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: those procedures --

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: be under any of our case law, that that would be inadequate per se?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Well, the circumst- under this Court's case law, what's required w- when the notice is served is determined by a consideration of a balance of factors in light of the circumstances.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, we've never...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: required actual notice in any case have we?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: No, although there is some suggestion of that in in a couple cases, for instance, Phillips Petroleum.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well but that's going to happen sometimes with any sort of notice except the requirement of actual receipt by the individual, and we've never felt that destroyed the validity of the notice.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: That's true, Your Honor, but in the circumstances presented here, consideration of the balance of factors leads to a requirement the procedures that would satisfy thao- those factors would also lead to actual notice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the delivery of the notice.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: with that if that had been the case here?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Even even though there was I was going to say even though there was no receipt signature, but I guess under the procedures now, the the prisoner would have signed a log.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Yes, he would have signed a log.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: The...

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: The procedure that was in place for certain kinds of mail that was labeled special mail if that had been if that had applied to notices in this category i- special mail, as I understand it, could be opened only in the presence of the prisoner?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: And if that had applied, that old rule had applied to this category of mail, you would not object to that, would you?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Well, the special mail o- opened only in front of the prisoners is happens after it's delivered to the prisoner.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Ms. Zieve, I'm I'm not sure what what is the general principle of which you assert the rule that you urge upon us in this case is is just an exemplar.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Your Honor, that's one important factor, but the case here is even easier because there are s-

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: there are numerous factors...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: not ur- urging that when the that that that the distinctive factor is just the Government is in charge of the delivery system because that would have been the case in the old post office when when the post office was actually part of the Federal Government.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: That's not the only factor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Here we have not --

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: what what is the principle wh- when the government is in charge of the residence? Right? So so so we would need a similar rule for all members of the armed forces.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: There are several key factors here, and I don't think you can separate out one circumstance from the rest.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: The inmate --

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Right? You you ch- I don't understand.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: If if...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: neither one alone is enough, I don't know why all in combination turn out to be enough.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Because that's the circumstances.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: The the procedures used for...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: apply to all members of the armed forces.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: The procedures used for delivery are also determined by the Government,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That would also...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: apply to the amed forces...

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: And the Government is in an adverse position, which I think requires additional cross checks in the system to ensure that care is taken because the Government doesn't have incentive to identify and rectify inefficiencies on its own.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: You really...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: think that that's realistic here? Y- You you would not urge us to apply this rule in a civil action where your client was being served a paper by by by someone who is not the Government?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Well, if the s- if the serving party were not the Government, then the person doing the service would not be the person who was also in control of the delivery...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I understand that. So, you have...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: not urged the rule in that case, only when the Government is is the opposing party.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Well, I'm not trying to be evasive, but it's hard to answer without knowing all the circumstances there.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: For instance, what would be the --

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: just like this one except that the complaint was not on behalf of the Government.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, then the...

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: private Individual wouldn't have control over the procedures...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: yes...

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: that mailing wasn't...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: requi-

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: wasn't adequate in that circumstance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: value of the property.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Do you assert that your rule would apply in that situation or not? All of the circumstances are the same.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Oh wait...

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: that the the prisoner owed money and the creditor brought this...

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: If it might not apply in that circumstance, but whether or not it does, this is an easier case.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, let let me ask you.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I'm going to ask the Government...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: the same thing I I I are we supposed to write an opinion that that says that mailed notice is adequate in any civil suit?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: think --

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: From a constitutional sta- I know what four E says, but from a constitutional standpoint, is mailed notice adequate in any civil suit?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Well, I think if probably not adequate in any civil suit.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: is it...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: what what is it that about this that makes mailed notice adequate whereas a- apparently there's another class of cases in which personal service is required?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Well, this...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: You you...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: you begin I I I think with the assumption that mailed notice would be adequate, and the question is, are these mailing procedures adequate in this case?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, no, Your Honor --

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: I'm I d- I don't want to have you take a position you don't take.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Not exactly, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: procedures after that.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: why is mail adequate at all? You seem to assume that, and that's fine.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: If the mail is received by the inmate?

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: any civil case.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: The mailed notice is any form of delivery any form of notice is adequate as to that individual if it's received.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Have any of our cases ever required certified mail?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Well, the first case where the Court considers whether mailed notices is constitutional in Hess versus Pawloski, the the mail at issue was certified mail.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: but never got there.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: one of the difficulties I find with your position Ms. Zieve is that this inquiry can come up, you know, months and maybe years after the actual notice took place or didn't take place.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Well, that's why the procedures should require proof of verification which the Government's procedures do today.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: received it...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I- we- u- we- v- sorry.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Under the circumstances.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, don't say under the s- there's no such thing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: The night --

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: calculated to give actual notice reasonably?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: The nineteen eighty-eight procedures did not provide assurances of delivery because what happened after the m- mail reached the mail room is was vague and undocumented.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: acknowledged...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I thought the proce- what what tell me precisely.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: The procedure was that the emp- mail room employee picked up the mail and signed the certified mail receipt at the post office, brought it back to the prison, where he entered it in a log book.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Mhm.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: A prison employee testified that in nineteen eighty-eight the procedure then would have been that a a housing unit staff would have signed when he took the mail the certified mail out of the mail room.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: He also testified that he didn't know what the procedure was after that.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: No. There was no testimony about that.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: d- described the procedure, and now you tell me what, in your opinion, is wrong with that procedure.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: The procedure doesn't require verification of delivery...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: and that --

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: opinion, it is unreasonable not to have an additional step that the prisoner signs for it.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Yes, and the reason is this.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Th- then we- su- whe- then whenever there's a requirement of s- or or a requirement procedure for a service by mail, it should be certified mail so the person signs for it in every case.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: In [NS]

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Ms. Zieve,

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: you couldn't say every case because in Mullane I- regular mail was adequate in that under the circumstances, which was th- heavily emphasized by Justice Jackson, those words, under the circumstances.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Th- in Mullane and in in some cases where there's a class of interested parties where everyone has this same interest, it might not be necessary for all interested parties to receive the notice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: and they all had identical interests --

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: question.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Well, that would likely be reasonable since the burden of doing so is so small and the increased th- the decreased risk of it not reaching the addressee would be...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I i- You you don't mean that it might be reasonable.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: not to do it.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: I- it wasn't good enough in in these circumstances.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, they they might --

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: they they might say more than that.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: i- e- Yeah, it would still mean the forfeiture was done...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: without adequate notice.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: so and which would not be the case, I suppose, unless you're going to adopt the certified mail rule, which would not be the case for an ordinary citizen who is not incarcerated.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Right?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: I don't know that it would be adequate notice to serve someone by mail out of prison if it's not received.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, what is -- what is...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: the statutory requirement in your view for forfeiture? As as far as notice is concerned?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: The statutory requirement? Is is...

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: publication and notice to the interested party.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: By mail? What what is the general statutory requirement on these forfeiture notices?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: I believe the statute says by mail, although for the Government always does it by certified mail.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: So...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: the materials we have in the briefs? Is there some copy of that provision somewhere that you're aware of in the briefs?

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Would not be certified.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Don't take a lot of time if you don't know.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I just thought...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: perhaps you knew.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: It's on page three of the Government's...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: brief.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: three of government's...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Page three.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: It just says, shall be sent to each party who appears to have an interest.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: See, that's why I don't know how to do it because, I mean, suppose there's a ship, for example.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: do...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: you have to get the signature? If if, for example, you're you're bringing an action against a thing and the thing is owned by millions, thousands, or hundreds, do th- does the ga- do do you have to normally, under the rules forget the Constitution for the moment you have to get the signature on a on th- on that notice, a return receipt requested of each person who has an interest in that thing?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Under the statute?

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Well now, you've asked a sort of complicated question because the statute for an in rem judicial...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: forfeiture...

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: requires publication,

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: but...

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: the Government, based on this Court's case law, gives notice by certified mail.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: take it you your argument would be that even if all the signatures we- are are not required in the in rem case, there is there is a fairness in the procedure that would not require actual notice and signatures to every ship owner because the owner of the ship at least has some right to control the ship.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: e- Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the procedures...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: as long as he wins.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: that. I mean.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: you would you would you would be satisfied if if we had the I I take it, the same kind of demand for proof of regularity for the period between the front door of the prison and the prisoner's cell that we do from the mailbox to the front door of the prison.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Probably.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: It would be nice to have a signature requirement, but basically we we would have the same kind of rule then that we have i- with with respect to mail delivery in general.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Yes, but the reason that I emphasize the signature is because, e- as the Bureau of Prisons has since recognized, the the signature does help to improve delivery.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: What do we do with prisoners who won't sign?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Well, actually the Board of Prisons procedures deal with that, and I think effectively, which is the on the log book, if the prisoner refuses to sign, then the person delivering the mail signs for it stating that the prisoner won't sign, which I think is comparable to a process server giving a contemporaneous statement that...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: service has been completed.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: to me we need to focus on some kind of a test, and I thought I understood you to argue for a test that says in these prison cases, there has to be actual receipt by the prisoner.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: They have to be adequate.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: if the Court...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: it isn't adequate unless there's actual n- receipt.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Yes, because those procedures are it's entirely practicable to do that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: difficult for...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: we don't agree with you, what's your fall-back position?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Well, even under the Third Circuit's formulation, the Court, c- looking at the nineteen eighty-eight procedures in the record, such as they are those procedures were inadequate.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well, why is it that a procedure is inadequate if the procedure is that if we get certified mail, we we deliver it to the prisoner? What's inadequate about that as a procedural standard?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Again, it doesn't provide the assurances of delivery that both are feasible because, if you're going to deliver it, it doesn't take that much to just get the signature, and because I think it's important to have cross checks to make sure that the Government...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Do we look back at Mullane or at Mathews versus Eldridge for our guidance here in establishing a standard?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: I think either one, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: inmates in its charge.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: rule to any setting other than prisons or are you ta- talking about the circumstances of someone incarcerated by the Government? Is there any other setting in which you would require not merely that certified mail be sent and signed for by someone, but in-hand delivery?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: There may be other cases, though there also may not be.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: knows where it is --

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: the easy case? It is the case, I take it from your argument, because you haven't suggested that there might be another setting other than where the Government has someone in confinement.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: No, I haven't.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Is it any part of your argument that we should be suspicious of prison officials because they may use a refusal to follow their procedures for vindictive reasons or anything like that?

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: That may occur, Your Honor, but our argument does...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: not rely on any malfeasance on the Government's part.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, if that's not part of your argument, I don't see why your situation is any different from the military situation where, unless the unless the serviceman or woman is AWOL, the Government knows r- right where that person is and the Government is responsible for for where that...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: person is in in many cases.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I don't know why you wouldn't have the same rule.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Well considered the military situation, and maybe there's more parallels.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Very well, Ms. Zieve.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: And...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: not just put the burden on the person who was supposed to get it to prove he didn't get it?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Well, that is in fact what the court did.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It didn't -- that wasn't a test it...

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: required, but the inquiry was made, and the court concluded that whatever the protestations of the defendant might be, or the prisoner in this case, nevertheless the method that was used here was reasonably calculated to reach him.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: requires.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: y- you you look, th- what what I take it that she's arguing for is there's a step missing here and it could a- should apply not just to prisoners, apply to anybody who's going to have their property forfeited, and that is, you give them notice by certified mail so they have to sign it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: The problem --

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: basically, we- as I understand it, the argument.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: The the problem with that position is it's contrary to fifty years of this Court's precedent, which has consistently recognized that mailing alone not certified mail but ordinary mail, is sufficient to provide parties with notice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Where did the Court hold that?

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: that with reference to a simple contract or tort action?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Nevertheless...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: why I'm asking because in in Mullane and subsequent cases, we have tended to say that the in rem in personam distinction is is not too clear a line.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: We are certainly s- willing to defend the proposition that the Federal Rules of Procedure, which...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: provide...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: I'm talking about due process c- requirements now.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I think  your question goes to the...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: If if this Court writes a due process opinion, can we say that mail, is or routine mail, is always sufficient, or are there some cases in which you must have personal service?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Well, the Court can certainly do that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: this case.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: the principle is that you're tha- that that controls our case, the the beginning principle here.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: The Court has drawn these principles largely from common experience and knowledge about the instrumentalities that are used for purposes of service.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: to Walk, to all of them.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: you are relying on th- the fact that this is a forfeiture case and we're ther- there's this voice of the past of in rem versus in personam hanging over this this argument of yours.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Well, n- actually no, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: providing notice.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: I'm I'm just very puzzled by your reliance on Mullane when Justice Jackson s- took took such care to say this procedure, common trust fund, so many beneficiaries, some unknown, some addresses lost if we use regular mail, the chances are it will get to many, if not most, people in the group, and that's good enough for that kind of case.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Your Honor, Mullane has been extended beyond the facts of its individual case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: might wish to make...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: probate proceeding.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: and whether ordinary --

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: I suppose in probate a claimant, a debtor claimant, is in much the same standing a- as as a person who sues in contract on that claim before the person dies.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: w- Well, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: case is...

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: doubt about the person who has the interest.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: But, Your Honor, the same could be said, for instance, in Mennonite with regard to the mortgagee, that the person who is at that that was a situation in which there was an interest in in foreclosing on a property and selling it at a tax sale.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: in that case.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: than publication generally.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: publication is is the least effective, and the Mullane case that you rely on so heavily makes that point.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yes, and that's the reason why the government in these situations provides notice by publication, also by notice to the person's last address, and also notice to his current address if it can be acertained In this case, the government, w- through region- reasonable diligence, was able to locate the individual and send notice to his place of incarceration.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Fine.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: The argument against that, Your Honor, is that the Due Process Clause simply specifies the constitutional minimum.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: process than is necessary.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: is, what is if you're in the government, aside from well, it's a little easier.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: The reason there there are several reasons for not recognizing a general constitutional standard.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: All right. And the...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: reason that that's wrong is because? I mean, th- the harm that it will do it other analogous situations.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: The rule would, first of all, require, for instance, as the Chief Justice has pointed out, similar nerv- er knowl- service on the armed services.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: It also would apply to other situations that might not be documented in the record.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: So, I want an example,

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: since you're writing the address anyway I want an example of the problem that would be caused by saying you not only have to write the address, you also have to send it certified, whether it's to dormitories, armed forces, prisoners, or anyone else in the world.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Well, I cannot say that the government cannot overcome that difficulty because it does in fact use certified mail.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: With certified mail, you have to get a r- signature from the recipient, whereas i- with ordinary mail, th- you can put it in the slot in your mailbox.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: example...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: it be difficult if you're going to take twenty thousand dollars away from them?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Bu- Your Honor, but again, this case is on on par with other cases that involved similar amounts of money.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: over assertions.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: the Third Circuit has apparently adopted a test that requires the government to show a little more than that it dropped a notice in the mail, and that, in fact, the government must show that procedures at the receiving facility, the prison, were reasonably calculated to deliver notice to the intended recipient.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Is that a a standard that the government would find satisfactory?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: We disagree with that, Your Honor, for the reasons set forth in Judge Alito's dissenting opinion on on that ground, namely, that it imposes burdens that are not necessary.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: been shown.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: your argument, it seems to me, is premised on the fact that forfeiture is like an in rem proceeding and it's just different.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: The reason...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: No-

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: that may be a a...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: heroic assumption.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I don't know of --

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: unavailability or the fact that the person is avoiding the process server or something like that?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: I don't know of any State that allows ordinary mail.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: mail with...

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: return receipt re- requested.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: method were based...

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: on that o- on that approach.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Well, we certainly do think it falls on the side of the th- of the line that this other c- that the other cases of this Court demarks, namely probate proceedings, tax sales, condemnation proceedings, notice forcible entry and detainer proceedings, such as which are basically e- e- ejectment proceedings.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: important policy --

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: ask you this prompted by Justice Scalia's question.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: I'm not aware of any special procedures that that we provide in those situations.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: For instance, if there was forfeiture --

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: and I should think there a lot of mail that might addressed to a serviceman might be signed for by the mail orderly or something.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: No.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: What about the the immunity while the person there's an immunity that governs people in the military during the time.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: The Soldier's and Sailor's Civil Relief Act.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yes, and I'm not sure how that would apply in this situation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: It would toll the statute...

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: of limitations because the person could assert immunity for the period that what- they're in service.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: ask you a question that puzzles me about this? The even before the current regulations, there was this special mail category and several things fell into it, including letters that the prisoner would get from the attorney.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: category called law enforcement.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: I think for a short while the DEA and the FBI did follow the practice of denominating certain mails as special mail.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: BOP...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Federal system, but the rule we're working with today would apply to State forfeitures as well, the State prisons and the like.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: No, they do not, although I think that the the the Court can can safely assume that State prisons do, in fact, provide for mail delivery to their prisoners.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Would you just comment on the suggestion that your opponent has made, in effect, that there really ought to be a special rule for people who are in the custody of the Federal Government? And I would assume with Justice Scalia that would include military personnel too.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Well, th- there's no reason to depart that's certainly the the fact that we know where the person is, certainly does dictate that we contact the person by mail, and that's inconsistent with Mullane and its progeny, that where the address is reasonably ascertainable the person should be contacted by mail.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: And if it does, it doesn't really matter if he's at a location of our choosing or some other location.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Is there any court action th- y- if- if you accepted your your points and said, look, they send it to the prison.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: I think not under the Tort Claims Act because it does exclude intentional torts.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: problem there --

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: type action.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Tha- under negligence? There is conceivably an an opportunity to seek damages based on negligence, although you'd have to show an absence of of due care.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: would satisfy the standard.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: position on one point.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Mr. Minear, am am I correct that the that the current method of simply providing written notice is you you maintain that that is method that Congress has specified in nineteen USC six sixteen oh seven A?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: The method of provi- the requirement of providing written notice I believe is in eighteen eighty-one, on page three of our briefs.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: seven A. I I'm...

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: mistaken.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: information -- on page...

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: three, and the fir- at the end of the first indented paragraph.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: mail --

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Shall be sent.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Shall be sent.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: To each party --

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: How how does one normally send things?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I mean,

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: normally by mail, and we think that...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: it's --

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Doesn't say shall be sent by certified mail.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: How do you think the Sixth Circuit standard differs from the Third Circuit's and the Fourth's?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: I think as a practical matter, Your Honor, there's primary primarily a question of the burden of proof.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well, but here the Government is seeking to forfeit property from someone.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Well, the Government met that burden in this case, but again, we don't think that that should be the test.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Mr. Minear, we have a smattering of courts of appeals cases addressing the issue that we've got today, but beyond that, I don't know how much litigation there is about this.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: I don't know the answer to that, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: which are so easily made.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: that I I don't know that the that that argument really favors you.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Your Honor, I think that you know, that the Department of Justice, and the Bureau of Prisons in particular, has tried to be helpful in that regard with re- with regard to providing this types of process.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: nineteen eighty-eight when this case arose.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: that there can be an institutional consideration in a closed case in deciding what due process does require?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: That certainly is a factor that the Court could weigh,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: but again --

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: problem if we if we held the way you've asked us to hold.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: There wouldn't be any litigation about that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Wouldn't be any litigation.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Well well, no.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Bu- no, there isn't, but...

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: there are also State prisons that do need to determine what procedures they would follow.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the inmate.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: it's not reasonable for the Government to assume that the State prison procedures are adequate to get mail to State prisoners.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: We think it is reasonably calculated.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Exactly, yes.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: we held that this is a reasonable sending it by mail is a reasonable way to do it, you wouldn't have any more of an institutional problem.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Well, no, we wouldn't.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: At the same --

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: we said that because there is a gap between mail delivery to a prison and delivery to a prisoner and, for that reason, there must be some indication of the procedure for getting the mail to the prisoner, then you might have a a problem and there might be an institutional advantage in a signature rule.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Well well, that's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: to the --

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: I don't understand why it's it's intuitively obvious that ordinary mail suffices when we have a situation here which is different from the situation covered by the the ordinary mail.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Your Honor, I think what what you see here is the the proceedings below validated the fact that ordinary mail would suffice, the fact that...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: there are mail --

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: as I understand it, did not stop with proof that the letter was mailed.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Well, perhaps the answer to your question, Your Honor, is that this Court can certainly affirm the decision below and say that the procedures below were adequate.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Mr. Minear, are there do you know whether there are more prisoners incarcerated in prisons than there are college students living in dormitories who to whom the mail is not delivered personally or or individuals living in high rise apartment buildings where the mail is is sorted downstairs and not delivered by the by the postman to their to their own room?

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: I do not know the answer to that...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: all those people, just as it is for prisoners.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Mr. Minear --

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: What about a hospital?

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: mail was not always routinely delivered to prisoners.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: again there are proce- --

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: just put it on the shelf and not even give it to the prisoner and there would be no remedy at all.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Obviously, if there is if there is reason to believe by the party that's sending notice the mail will not that ordinary mailers will not suffice, then due process may require that additional steps be taken.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Minear, w- in the in the hospital setting, the Government has a claim against someone, knows that person is hospitalized, sends ordinary mail to the hospital for the contract claim, the tort claim, whatever.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Your Honor, I think that that it would for this reason, that if a return address is provided and the mail is not delivered by the hospital, we can expect the hospital to send the mail return it to sender.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: under an obligation at that point --

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I mea- suppose...

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: all we know is that this mail well, I think your time is up.

turns.name: JEFFREY P. MINEAR
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Mr. Minear.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: [NS] First of all, I'd like to address the the suggestion that these cases are about false claims.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: proc-

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Ms. Zieve.

turns.name: ALLISON M. ZIEVE
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: The case is submitted.
